reasoning_model_1: The user requested the model to read the specific sentence 'Haha, you're really hilarious!' with laughter and a medium pace. Model 1 failed significantly in Content Accuracy. Instead of reading only the target sentence, it read the sentence followed by a long, hallucinated description of the scene and style ('...said with a medium pace and a grin, like you just told a joke...'). Regarding Non-Linguistic Vocalizations, the model simply read the word 'Haha' textually rather than generating a genuine sound of laughter. Regarding Pace, while the speaking rate itself was medium, the inclusion of substantial extraneous text makes the generation completely incorrect according to the scoring standard for Content Accuracy (0 = Voice content is clearly inconsistent with the script).
reasoning_model_2: Model 2 followed the instructions very well. In terms of Content Accuracy, it delivered the core message 'You are really hilarious', treating the text 'Haha' as a direction for the Non-Linguistic Vocalization. Consequently, the audio begins with a natural, genuine sound of laughter rather than the spoken word 'Haha', which perfectly satisfies the 'with laughter' constraint. The Pace is measured and medium, sounding natural and clearly articulated. The transition from laughter to speech is fluid. It effectively conveys the requested features without any content errors or hallucinations.
model_comparison: Model 1 is a failure case as it suffers from severe prompt leakage/hallucination, reading out descriptive text ('said with a medium pace...') instead of stopping at the target sentence. It also failed to produce actual non-linguistic laughter, merely reading the word 'Haha'. Model 2, in contrast, correctly interpreted the command. It generated a realistic non-linguistic laugh at the beginning (mapping to 'Haha') and delivered the rest of the sentence with a steady, medium pace. Model 2 is superior in all dimensions.
